data.title: Mastercard: More Than A Payment Network, And Still Undervalued
data.description:
Thesis

Mastercard represents an attractive buying opportunity, with increasingly scrutinous regulations and growing competition concealing the silent transition to a data/service-driven business. Along with this, a rebound in cross-border transactions and an already asset-light model justify current premiums in forward multiples. For these reasons, I am assigning a Buy rating with a ~40% potential upside, as I see such points playing out in the long term.

Company Overview

Mastercard operates a worldwide technology network that facilitates electronic transactions between consumers, institutions, businesses, and others. Most of their products fall into 3 main segments:

Payment Processing: The company acts as an intermediary, securely routing transaction data between parties, ensuring the right banks, businesses, and institutions have access to the right funds.

Network Operations: Mastercard supports credit, debit, and prepaid card transactions (you will often see its logo on these cards), facilitating EFTs (Electronic Fund Transactions) among different parties.

Security & Redundancy: Provide threat detection, AI analytics, and cybersecurity solutions to defend companies and consumers from data breaches and fraud.

The company operates on an entirely electronic scale, having virtually no COGS or inventory. They act solely as an intermediary and electronic router/service provider (2025 10-K Page 23), enabling payments for around 150 million merchants worldwide. This contributes to the asset-light model and operating leverage, which will be explained later. Mastercard has a roughly 60/40 split (2025 10-K Page 56) in terms of revenue, with 60% coming from its payment processing segment (core to the business, slower growth) and 40% from value-added services (security, consulting, loyalty programs). Services are the fastest-growing segment, with a shift in business model becoming more apparent in recent years. For reference, value-added services grew 26% in 2025, more than doubling that of payment processing (12%).

The important thing to take away here lies in the sustained and elevated revenue growth we've seen, even in recent years. This growth story will be important for the valuation of the company, as I believe we will continue to see sustained growth into the medium term, as Mastercard has not yet saturated its market for value-added services.

Services: The True Value Driver

As pictured in the graph below, value-added services have outpaced payment processing in terms of revenue growth for the past 5 years, with the disparity becoming clearer as cybersecurity threats and AI-based analytics become more relevant with the advent of powerful AI models. Just this past week, Anthropic announced that it would hold off the release of its newest model, "Mythos," because it was too adept at identifying software weaknesses in even the most advanced security/browsing systems. As these tools become more relevant, we continue to see a disconnect between the 2 segments, with services growing at a rate more than double that of payment processing in 2025.

To understand why this is happening, we can take a look at why current executives are choosing Mastercard when it comes to security, consulting, and other software/service-based needs. The company is already supporting large institutions with their own security and consulting teams, providing strategic and tangible value to multiple clients, some of whom already use other products, such as Payment Processing, from Mastercard as well. Not only does its position as a processing giant place it in a good position to cement trust in clients already used to doing business with them, but such offerings also ensure a sticky, recurring, SaaS-like operating model, with higher margins and larger growth rates than those of core banking/processing fees.

Along with this, Mastercard Services, the subsidiary of Mastercard that provides the services I've mentioned before, has achieved a CSAT (Customer Satisfaction) rating of ~79% according to the most recent reports. This is slightly below Visa (82%) but well above other competitors such as PayPal (64%), making Mastercard Services an above-average solutions arm, able to compete with top-tier payment networks already ingrained in the segment. This has led to a nearly 2% market share gain in the services segment over the 2021-2025 period, a significant amount for an already saturated and high-volume market (with larger companies like Visa).

A steady increase in market share, along with the sustained double-digit revenue growth we've seen for the past 5 years, leads me to believe that services are and will continue to be the main value driver for the company. Although in its infancy when compared to other companies within the segment, Mastercard Services will continue to innovate (AI, agentic solutions, cybersecurity) its products faster than competitors. The market for the services segment is relatively unsaturated compared to its processing counterpart, meaning Mastercard can leverage its enormous network of clients to implement and push solutions towards clients that already have relationships with and trust the company, meaning this growth story has likely just begun.

Cross-Border Transactions: A Network Effect Yet To Be Realized

Cross-border transactions, those being international e-commerce, global payroll, cross-currency business investments, and otherwise, are experiencing a significant rebound in 2025-2026, owed mainly to a 14% increase in global foreign direct investment (FDI) to around 1.6 trillion in 2025. Mainly driven by M&A/financial services (129% growth), the increase in FDI has led to a near 15% increase in YoY cross-border transaction volume for Mastercard over the same period. This trend shows no sign of stopping, with cross-border transaction volume estimated to reach ~320 trillion in 2032, nearly doubling from 194.6 trillion in 2024.

The effect this trend has is cyclical in nature. As transaction volume and revenue grow, merchants will be forced to accept Mastercard if they don't already in order to capture spending in tourist-heavy areas, where most business and spending originate. As Mastercard is accepted by more merchants, more consumers will adopt such cards due to ease of use and trust through brand name recognition. This network effect will be multi-market, owed to the nature of the transactions themselves. Further, cross-border transactions carry higher fees and require risk handling, meaning the highest-margin revenue will likely see the largest amount of growth.

However, it is important to note that most cross-border cash flows are corporate-driven and are driving the growth that we've seen in FDI. Mastercard is also poised to benefit from this interaction, given the implications increased deal flow has for other sections of corporate financials. With an increase in deal volume comes an uptick in intercompany transfers, supplier payments, and global payroll. As firms expand, they rely on card networks such as Mastercard for scalable payment solutions like commercial cards and cross-border transaction networks, both of which Mastercard has already integrated into its business model. Regarding payroll, 18% of cross-border payroll transactions experience FX discrepancies, which is just one example of where Mastercard comes in (FX conversion, transactions/ongoing reconciliation).

Therefore, Mastercard stands to gain not just from growth in cross-border consumer spending but also from B2B/M&A transaction volume as we see a rebound in both following a post-covid shift towards digital payments that make it easier to travel, buy, and acquire across borders. The company finds itself at the epicenter of both markets, owing to its already integrated business model and longstanding relationships with both consumers and businesses, who trust and will continue to trust it for anything from currency conversion to global payroll and everything in between.

Operating Leverage + Asset-Light Model: The Path To Earnings Power

Mastercard is certainly one of the most scalable business models in the current market, for multiple reasons. The company operates on a large fixed-cost base (~$12-14 billion yearly), allowing it to have extremely low incremental revenue costs per transaction (around 3.9 cents in 2021). This gives it extreme scalability, meaning expenses will almost always increase at a rate below revenue growth, a trend exacerbated by the sustained double-digit revenue growth seen in recent years. As transaction volume grows, it is likely we will continue to see Mastercard benefit from such trends, as it can scale its model with little to no increase in incremental costs.

Pictured above are both the operating margin and ROA from 2021 to 2025, seeing a 4.24% and 6.72% increase, respectively. For a business already operating on the highest margins in its industry, this is an extraordinary feat, and one that could only be achieved through a scalable business unlike any other. Even Visa, one of Mastercard's closest competitors and a giant in the industry, has seen its own operating margin decrease by ~6%, from 66.64% to 60.49%, over the same timeframe. This is not an industry-wide tailwind; it is a systematic scaling and expansion of operating leverage that few other companies can match.

Of course, there is upfront investment needed with payment rails, data centers, and otherwise, but once this infrastructure is built, there is no physical product, no inventory, and no logistics required, as payments/transactions merely involve moving data packets across networks (costing pennies at a time). It is nearly impossible to build this level of scale from scratch, as it requires acceptance from both merchants and users at the same time, with one often requiring the other. Mastercard and other industry giants have built these effects over 50+ years, making such scale almost impossible to replicate and giving Mastercard a wide moat.

When compared to comps, Mastercard also maintains the highest Asset Turnover Ratio when compared to both the industry average and some of its closest competitors, namely Visa. This should come as no surprise given the asset-light model and focus on margin expansion, as Mastercard is significantly more efficient with the use of assets to produce sales, owing to the fixed-cost infrastructure base and low incremental costs of revenue as described earlier. This will continue to drive the margin expansion that we've seen in prior years, and as transaction volume worldwide continues to grow with the advent of digital payments, I believe Mastercard is well-placed to drive growth within its operating segments and justify the premiums its multiples currently imply.

Financials

Regarding margins, as stated earlier, Mastercard retains a strong GAAP and adjusted operating margin, those being 58.3% and 55.8% in 2025, representing a substantial increase YoY from 52.6% in 2024. This trend will likely continue as we see elevated revenue growth growing at rates disproportionate to operating expenses, owed to the scalability that I talked about earlier. With nearly $16.3 billion in levered cash flow during 2025, along with strong cash flow conversion rates, Mastercard has a seemingly low-risk capital structure, with a solid amount of cash flow relative to its total debt load ($18.9 billion).

Revenue growth was robust in 2025, with services being the fastest-growing segment at ~22% revenue growth YoY compared to ~12% in the payment processing segment, with both segments bringing total revenue to $32.79 billion in 2025, representing a 16.74% YoY increase from 2024. Growth continues to be driven by cross-border transaction volume as described earlier, with higher fees (0.6-3%+ vs. 1.5-2%) driving the mix shift (despite only making up 1/6 of total volume).

We will also likely continue to see growth in EPS ($13.98 in 2024 to $16.20 in 2025), especially given increased buybacks and reduced share count (-6% in 2024 to -8% in 2025), enhancing EPS beyond normal net income growth. An extremely high-margin business model and possession of scalability seen by only a few other institutions give Mastercard a wide moat to invest in both its business and shareholders. With the service segment driving growth and current model/macro tailwinds following suit, Mastercard will likely have the financial power it needs to meet and beat expectations as we look toward the long term.

Valuation

The current stock price is $498.66, as of market close on 12 April. Using a perpetuity growth rate of 2% and a calculated WACC of 8.89% (see below), I reached an implied share price of $695.54, representing a 39.4% upside from the current price. Commenting on the terminal growth rate, it is rather conservative for a company currently seeing double-digit growth. But my model already has the terminal value making up nearly 75% of total enterprise value, meaning it already relies heavily on long-term assumptions, not near-term cash flows. In order to temper this effect and ensure a fair valuation, I found it necessary to lower the terminal growth rate to achieve this effect.

Revenue growth begins at elevated levels seen in recent years, tapering off quickly towards the terminal year as we trim towards the perpetuity growth rate. I see OpEx growing at around 6-8% YoY, with most other line items remaining stable or around this same rate, in order to represent both the averages of historical years and the scalability integrated into the current business model. EBITDA margins will expand by around 3.8%, again, conservative for a company that saw a 3.3% increase just last year, but necessary to avoid over-assumption of long-term growth. Actual sales growth may exceed current assumptions put into the model, but conservatism is needed given the current premium Mastercard sells at, both in terms of P/E ratio and forward multiples, which will be explained below.

Peer Analysis & Competition

Due to the duopoly nature of the sector Mastercard is in (Mastercard & Visa control nearly 80-85% of the market), it was difficult to find companies of similar market cap, exact business model, or otherwise. That being said, there are many similar businesses, those being AmEx (premium card services), PayPal (FinTech and wallet-first), and more that are relatively similar in business model and otherwise. The methodology/criterion I adhered to is as follows:

  • Business Model/Revenue Drivers
  • Market Capitalization
  • Forward Multiples (EV/EBITDA, EV/Sales)
  • Margin/Capital Structure and Other Metrics

You will notice that Mastercard, AmEx, and Visa have larger market caps than most other competitors, owing to phenomena and circumstances explained earlier. However, averages are mostly in line with what can be expected of the comp set, as I tried to bring together companies from across all walks of the sector while keeping ratios/statistics relatively similar. Mastercard has margins ~25% higher than that of the comp set median and a sales CAGR from 25% to 27%, nearly 3% higher than that of the median as well.

For these reasons, I believe the premiums that Mastercard currently trades at on forward multiples (EV/EBITDA at 11.5x compared to 9.4x average, similar story for EV/Sales) are justified given expected growth rates, a wide moat, and the scalability of its model. Mastercard certainly faces high expectations, but with current growth drivers and tailwinds, I have the utmost confidence that we will soon see these multiples justified as Mastercard continues to produce sustained growth and returns to investors.

Risks

As I said earlier, Mastercard operates in a duopoly with Visa in the credit card industry, exposing it to extreme regulatory scrutiny and antitrust concerns regarding fees and customer satisfaction, especially as of late. Proposed legislation such as the Credit Card Competition Act that aims to put caps on fees, along with increased FTC scrutiny, targets Mastercard's core model and puts revenue growth expectations in danger. However, with the shift towards services and redundancy of core operating segments being enacted, along with the lobbying/settlement efforts already occurring in court, it is clear that Mastercard is actively fighting to ensure it meets expectations for investors and maintains its current business model well into the future.

Cross-border sensitivity and emerging markets are also issues that contend with current thesis points. In my last article on Fiserv, I discussed the emergence of stablecoins and how they could revolutionize the current cross-border transaction ecosystem. This poses a risk to the current order we see now, meaning Mastercard and other card networks have to innovate and expand operations to ensure relevancy. Along with this, revenue-based sensitivity (highest margin segment) means Mastercard could lose big on any shift in current spending trends. Diversification away from pure consumer dependence (B2B transactions) and bridging stable coins into its own network will make relevancy an easier task as we advance into the digital age of payments.

A more long-term risk is posed by competition even outside of Mastercard's own sector. The risk of transactions completely bypassing Mastercard's networks (account-to-account payments, real-time payment rails, etc.) poses a threat to the entirety of Mastercard's model, threatening to render it irrelevant as governments/banks introduce entirely new alternatives to current transaction/payment methods. M&A (acquiring these alternatives), along with embedding the value-added services into its core streams, will help weather this storm into the future as Mastercard faces stiff competition both in and outside its industry.

Final Thoughts

Mastercard is not cheap, and it certainly has a high bar to meet for both investors and clients when it comes to its operating segments. However, such premiums are justified, as Mastercard finds itself at the forefront of both macro and fundamental tailwinds in the near/long term. Using the financial leverage provided by the nature of its model, along with efficient utilization of assets to push already double-digit growth segments, I believe that Mastercard has the tools it needs to beat investor expectations and provide appreciation for the foreseeable future.

data.title: War, Inflation - No Problem; Next Challenge - Earnings
data.description:
By Geoff Bysshe

After intense negotiations over the weekend with Iran, the end result has been an announcement that the U.S. will blockade the Strait of Hormuz.

A few weeks ago, this news would have likely sent the stock market tumbling. Now, however, it will likely be viewed as little more than a speed bump, as the market races into earnings season.

The market’s message last week was very clearly one that looked through the risks of elevated oil prices and the Iran war, and focused on the risks and benefits of the AI revolution across various sectors of the economy.

Stocks Put The War On The Back Burner

In last week’s Market Outlook, we highlighted the market rotation that was forming a bottom and economic news that could support a more bullish market narrative.

This was supported by the fact that the bond market had also stopped going down in the face of higher crude prices and other news that would normally put pressure on rates to move higher.

The market was ready to rally before the ceasefire news, so the welcome catalyst created a strong rally that held up and followed through in the indexes and several key sectors.

The war’s market bottom is in. Now it’s up to earnings to keep the bull market intact. With earnings taking center stage, the economy, interest rates, and inflation will be the supporting cast of characters.

Wall St. vs. Consumers On Inflation

Everyone is expecting higher oil prices to lead to higher inflation in the near term, and Friday’s CPI data proved this to be true.

As you can see in the month-over-month CPI chart below, last month's data jumped significantly.

Shown below, the year-over-year measure also has an accelerating look to it, as it jumped to the highest level since early 2024.

If the chart below were of a stock’s price, the technician would surely suggest there is strong support at the 2.5% level, and the bounce suggests more upside ahead and little chance we’ll see the Fed’s 2% target anytime soon.

Typically, higher inflation leads to higher interest rates, potentially slower growth, pressure on stock multiples, and more reason for stocks to retreat. If the cause of inflation is consumer demand, then stocks have a justification for moving higher, but that's not the case here.

However, the big monthly jump of 0.9% in CPI inflation didn’t faze stocks, bonds, or expectations for Fed rate cuts in 2026.

The muted reaction by the markets is most likely because:

  1. Markets were expecting 1.0%, so the high number was actually not as bad as feared.
  2. The primary driver of the inflation was the price of gasoline, and as we discussed in last week’s article and as already noted this week, the market seems to be treating higher oil prices as somewhat transitory and manageable even at elevated levels vs. pre-war prices.

Looking at the Fed Funds market’s odds of rate changes, you can see that there were no significant probability changes as a result of any of last week’s news, including the CPI data.

The table below shows the odds data at the end of last week and the week prior. The blue shading shows the rate with the highest probability for the given Fed meeting. The meetings that show any changes are in 2027, and even the odds within the blue boxes did not change meaningfully.

The Consumer Disagrees

While the markets seem complacent about inflation, the consumer sees it differently. The University of Michigan Survey of Consumers’ expectations for inflation one year into the future reported a value of 4.8%, which is much higher than the 4.2% expected and breaks a long downtrend in expectations.

Is The Bull Market Safely Over The 200-day Moving Average?

The 200-day moving average is viewed by many as the mark of a significant correction. Several weeks ago, we wrote about the changing nature of market behavior when it’s below the 200-day. Breaking below the 200-day is predictably bearish.

Likewise, recovering from being under the 200-day is predictably bullish. You do not, however, want to be too quick to claim the bearish move is over.

Since the market was able to recently close over 1% higher than the 200-day for 2 days, I looked at what history says about the SPY when it breaks the 200-day by at least 3%, and then what happens after it recovers with 2 days of closing 1% above the 200-day.

For the discretionary trader with some decent trading rules like our “close and continue” tactic, this recovery criterion is very effective. There are a few instances where 1% wasn’t enough to sustain a rally, but it is enough for the nimble trader who adapts to a reversal back into negative territory.

It is certainly prudent to wait for markets to recover the 200-day level if you want to avoid the biggest drawdowns. Below, you’ll find the stats of the last 23 occurrences of this pattern.

Before we look at the summary, take note of the following.

  • Our recent break and recovery was only a 3.98% drawdown below the 200-day, and it recovered in 8 days, which is tied with the fastest recovery time going back to 1994. The last 8-day recovery fell back under the sell signal within a month.
  • There is only one instance when the market was negative after 5 days, and it proceeded to be positive in 20 days. And that negative value was only -0.12%. This implies that the next “confirmation” of a sustained rally could be that the market has moved up in the 5 days since its trigger of turning bullish.

The summary statistics below do show the pattern is worth paying attention to.

When you look at the charts, it becomes clear that the markets that break up through the 200-day without quickly retreating tend to lead to substantial gains. The failures are also quite remarkable. So this is a very tactical trade setup. Use risk management.

The Next Obstacle For The Bulls Is Earnings Season

This week, the banks kick off earnings season. Expectations are high, and investors are eager to hear what management has to say about how the war is impacting their outlook.

Below you’ll find a table of major bank announcement dates this week.

The Stars Ranking is our PRIME ranking (1-5) for the technical condition of the trend in 3 different time frames. The short term (1-5 days) is too short to be relevant for an earnings announcement, but the intermediate term (2-8 weeks) and the long term (2-6+ months) are readings that could be helpful.

If the earnings announcement pushes a stock with an intermediate- or long-term bullish 4- or 5-star reading, that momentum could follow through. On the other hand, a weak earnings report on a stock with only 2 or 3 stars could send it much lower.

Best wishes for your trading.

Big View Summary

By Keith Schneider

Every week, we review the big picture of the market's technical condition as seen through the lens of our Big View data charts.

The bullets provide a quick summary organized by conditions we see as being risk-on, risk-off, or neutral.

Summary: Markets staged a broad-based risk-on rally with strong gains, improving breadth, leadership from semiconductors and growth, and multiple indicators (internals, volume, risk gauges, global equities, and Bitcoin) flipping bullish - though momentum still faces resistance at declining intermediate trends.

However, conditions are now stretched to near-term overbought levels, with volatility compressing and macro inputs (rates, commodities, gold) largely neutral, suggesting the rally may need consolidation before sustaining higher.

Risk-On

  • Markets put in a strong week, up 3-4.5% on the week, with three of the four closing back in bull phases. One concern is that momentum just got back up to its declining 50-Day Moving Average on real motion and remains in a bear phase. (+)
  • Sectors were nearly positive across the board, with the exception of IBB, which was flat and which came off its highest levels. Semiconductors were up over 11%, and consumer discretionary also had a strong week. It was the worst week for oil prices in six years. (+)
  • Volume patterns clearly favored risk-on, with all indexes having more accumulation days than distribution days. (+)
  • Speculative sectors like semiconductors and related countries were the best performers this week. (+)
  • The 52-week new high new low ratio had a major reversal and flipped positive with a positive stack and slope. (+)
  • The color charts (moving average of stocks above key moving averages) on a short-term basis across all the indexes are flipping to positive bull mode readings with improving long-term readings. (+)
  • Risk gauges have flipped fully positive. (+)
  • The value vs. growth relationship is showing growth is regaining some short-term leadership and improving its phase, while value returned to a bull phase. (+)
  • The Modern Family was strong this week, with 5 of the six members up on the week and SMH, KRE, and IWM reclaiming a bullish phase. Semiconductors hit a new all-time high. (+)
  • Foreign equities resumed leadership, and both emerging and developed markets are in bull phases. (+)
  • Bitcoin showed some strength, clearing its 50-Day Moving Average for the first time in several months. (+)
  • Seasonal trends are good heading into April for the S&P and Nasdaq, though still weaker for small caps. (+)

Neutral

  • Market internals surged to overbought levels this week from oversold levels two weeks ago. This could be an overall positive momentum thrust. The NASDAQ is a bit more extreme than the S&P. (=)
  • The color charts (moving average of stocks above key moving averages) on a short-term basis across all the indexes have or are flipping to positive bull mode readings. (+)
  • Volatility backed off considerably from its peak on March 27th. The Cash VIX is back into a warning phase and testing support. (=)
  • Soft commodities eased a bit this week, however, we did get a cross of the 50-Day Moving Average over the 200-Day Moving Average. DBA is lagging the SPY on a short-term basis. (=)
  • Gold was up marginally on the week, while oil came off its highest levels. (=)
  • Rates remained stable this week. (=)

Actionable Trading Plan

Bias: Lean Risk-On, but tactically managed (not fully committed yet)

  • Increase equity exposure incrementally rather than all at once, prioritizing leadership areas (semiconductors, growth, discretionary, and strong foreign markets).
  • Avoid laggards (biotech, soft commodities) unless they show clear relative strength improvement.

Execution Framework

  • Buy strength on continuation, not weakness - focus on names/sectors holding gains after the sharp rally rather than chasing extended moves.
  • Scale in over multiple entries to reduce risk of buying into a short-term overbought condition.
  • Favor relative strength leaders making new highs or reclaiming bull phases (SMH-type leadership, IWM/KRE confirmation, Bitcoin above key trend).

Risk Management / Positioning

  • Keep position sizes moderate given overbought internals and resistance at declining intermediate momentum.
  • Maintain a cash buffer (20-40%) to stay flexible if the rally stalls or reverses.
  • If leadership sectors begin to fail broadly (not just pull back), reduce exposure quickly rather than waiting for confirmation.

What to Watch (Decision Triggers)

Follow-through vs. stall:

  • Continued expansion in new highs and strong sector breadth → add exposure.
  • Loss of momentum + failed breakouts → shift back toward neutral.Leadership health:
  • If semis/growth continue leading → stay risk-on.
  • If rotation shifts defensively → tighten risk.

    Volatility behavior:
  • Stable/declining volatility → supports adding risk.
  • Sharp volatility spike → move to defense quickly.

Bottom Line

Participate in the upside, but treat this as an early-stage regime shift with overbought risk - build exposure selectively, stay focused on leaders, and be quick to de-risk if momentum fails.

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Wall Street Lunch: Netflix Draws Bullish Backing As Ad Revenue Seen Doubling To $3B
data.description:
Listen below or on the go on Apple Podcasts and Spotify

Netflix gains bullish calls as ad revenue growth outlook strengthens. (0:15) Goldman pressured after weaker net interest income and higher provisions. (1:30) Existing home sales falll as affordability and labor weakness weigh. (1:52)

This is an abridged transcript of the podcast:

Our top story so far, with the pursuit of Warner Bros. Discovery (WBD) behind it and a recent price hike absorbed by subscribers, Netflix (NFLX) is emerging as a lower-volatility business with expanding advertising potential. Morgan Stanley and Wedbush both reiterated bullish views and raised their price targets.

Wedbush expects Netflix’s ad revenue to at least double to $3B this year, citing opportunities to expand partnerships, refine targeting, leverage AI and add more live content. Analyst Alicia Reese reiterated an Outperform rating and lifted her price target 3% to $118, pointing to pricing power and interactive ads as incremental growth drivers.

Morgan Stanley’s Sean Diffley also highlighted additional upside from advertising, alongside benefits from recent price increases and the end of the WBD pursuit. He said engagement continues to strengthen, with hours watched approaching 200B annually, supported by live and sports programming that drives “must-see” moments.

Diffley reiterated an Overweight rating and raised his price target 4.5% to $115, calling Netflix the dominant player in premium streaming content despite broader concerns about engagement growth.

Among active stocks, Goldman Sachs (NYSE:GS) stock is under pressure after the Wall Street bank's Q4 net interest income fell short and its provision for credit losses came in higher than consensus estimates.

The bottom line, though, exceeded expectations, helped by strong top-line results in its Global Banking & Markets division, which offset a Q/Q dip in Wealth Management revenue.

Looking to the economy, U.S. existing home sales were down 3.6% M/M to 3.98M, vs. 4.08M consensus and 4.13M prior (revised from 4.090M).

Pantheon Macro says the March numbers, which mostly will reflect sales initially agreed in February, suggest the soft labor market, slower population growth, depressed confidence, and stretched affordability all are suppressing the boost to the housing market from the earlier drop-in mortgage rates.

In other news of note and in the Wall Street Research Corner, Seeking Alpha identified a basket of U.S. dividend stocks that offer a compelling mix of income generation and financial strength.

All selected companies have earned dividend grades between B- and A+ across key factors such as yield, growth, consistency, and safety.

Among the stocks are Bristol-Myers Squibb (BMY), Omnicom Group (OMC), First American Financial (FAF), Texas Instruments (TXN) And AbbVie (ABBV).

data.title: Axsome Is Priced For Perfection Ahead Of Auvelity's Alzheimer's Agitation Decision
data.description:
Axsome Overview

Despite my past bearishness, Axsome (AXSM) has outperformed the market.

My caution stems from Axsome’s undifferentiated products in crowded markets and the costs associated with marketing them.

Auvelity (Major Depressive Disorder), for instance, is a clever combination of a known antidepressant (bupropion) and dextromethorphan. It is one of over a dozen antidepressants in a market full of dirt-cheap generics.

Sunosi is one of several medicines used to treat excessive daytime sleepiness due to narcolepsy or obstructive sleep apnea.

And Symbravo is, again, a clever combination of a known “anti-migraine,” rizatriptan, and a known pain reliever, meloxicam, for acute migraines.

Of course, everything must be viewed in the context of valuation, and Axsome now sports a $9B valuation on these three undifferentiated products plus its pipeline.

Now, I have lauded Auvelity’s (AXS-05) prospects in Alzheimer’s Disease Agitation, or ADA, in the past. I do think Auvelity will be a differentiated product in the ADA market specifically. Axsome is approaching an April 30, 2026 PDUFA in this indication. The company is hopeful too, doubling their sales team ahead of the big day.

However, circling back to Axsome’s premium valuation, I’m going to side with caution in AXSM, once more, because the stock appears to already reflect meaningful approval optimism.

Auvelity

The ADA market size can be a bit misleading at a glance. There are over 7 million AD patients in the US. About half of these patients, over time, will have symptoms of agitation. And as I’ve pointed out in the past, there is a push towards non-pharmacologic approaches in dementia care. And although Auvelity could be favored as a non-antipsychotic oral option (relative to Rexulti and off-label drugs), the Medicare Part D population will likely entail step-therapy and prior authorization from payers.

It’s likely that Auvelity expands the ADA agitation market. But I don’t think Auvelity will own it. There is an expectation that prescribers will continue to use Rexulti “because docs are familiar with it.”

What about the odds the FDA will approve it? Drugs at the NDA stage have ~90% odds of approval. Since Auvelity’s safety is long-established, one could even assume higher odds.

However, Auvelity’s efficacy in ADA is mixed. One of four clinical trials (ADVANCE-2), a parallel-group, 5 week trial, failed to show benefit (CMAI: -13.8 vs -12.6).

Overall, the package is, at the least, supportive, and Auvelity has Fast Track, Breakthrough Therapy, and Priority Review in ADA.

Regarding pricing, Auvelity currently costs ~$1k-$1.5k in MDD. We can expect modestly higher pricing in ADA.

I have Launch Year as 2027 (first full year on market), Peak at 2032, and LOE at 2037. I went with a Target Pop. of 350k with 3.5% annual Pop. Growth and Peak Share of 12%. Expenses include 8% COGS, 45% Launch OpEx (3 Yrs), and 35% Steady-State OpEx.

In ADA, I have Auvelity worth $1.25B in risk-adjusted Net Present Value, generating ~$800M in peak annual revenue. Assuming it is approved, the NPV would jump to $1.39B.

I understand that my estimates are close to “bear case” scenarios proposed by Cowen. Others, like Truist, see Auvelity generating $3B at peak.

Personally, I would place my $800M as the “prove-it” base case, but with a real possibility of generating $1.6B/yr at peak if Peak Share approaches 20%. It’s going to really boil down to prescriber and payer habits, which are less predictable than clinical need.

That said, we do have Rexulti, despite its baggage (boxed warning for increased mortality) as a direct example of what an ADA drug can generate in revenue. Rexulti made $972M (converted from DKK) in total revenue for Lundbeck in FY25 with ADA scripts at roughly 20% of total US Rexulti volume. This implies ~$200M in ADA-specific revenue two years after approval.

Launching Auvelity in ADA should be pretty easy for Axsome. They’ve built relationships with the same prescriber class for years in MDD. That said, meaningfully penetrating long-term care facilities will be a bit different. And despite its obvious safety advantages, prescriber inertia could work against a new entrant.

Financial Health

Axsome appears to be "turning a corner" these last eight quarters.

What’s most encouraging, to me, has been the drop in SG&A Burden, implying that its sales infrastructure investments are paying off. And, again, I suspect that a clean ADA win should be what’s needed to get Axsome to a cash-generative state.

Looking ahead to Axsome’s Q1 report, expected 5/4/2026, investors will want to see more inflection and operating leverage.

Axsome ended 2025 with $322.9M in cash and cash equivalents, which they believe is good enough to get them to “cash flow positivity.”

Let’s suppose that Axsome achieves 15% FCF Margin on average over the next nine years. What revenue growth would be needed to support its $9B valuation?

Year 1 Revenue of $1B is between the consensus revenue estimates (n=21) of $905M and $1.06B for 2026.

I modeled for 30% base Revenue Growth (realized 27.6% CAGR) with a High Growth profile (Yrs 1–3: 45.0% → Yrs 4–6: 30.0% → Yrs 7–9: 15.0%).

Stock beta varies by source. Because Axsome’s discount rate is equity-heavy (it has very little debt), if I were to plug in a basic "stock beta," the discount rate would be unrealistically low. A modeled Beta of 1.2 (industry-adjusted) results in a WACC Discount Rate of 10.25%, which appears appropriate for a mid-cap pharma company.

Most everything else is either standard DCF methodology (Terminal Growth of 2%) or was derived from Axsome’s most recent financial statements.

Even with optimistic assumptions of 30% base Revenue Growth and 15% FCF Margins, AXSM is “overvalued” by roughly 17%.

Per a Reverse DCF Analysis, a few things could “marry” the DCF Value per Share with the Current Stock Price.

Pipeline

A common nuance with DCF models is that the majority of total enterprise value is actually made up of terminal (in this case, Year 10 and beyond) assumptions. For Axsome, the question becomes how it’ll make money post-Auvelity LOE.

Attention then turns to Axsome’s pipeline. But as I’ve discussed in the past, there isn’t much that excites me beyond Auvelity in ADA.

Axsome is hoping to expand Sunosi’s reach beyond EDS into conditions like ADHD, MDD with EDS, and BED. But I don’t envision any blockbuster opportunities from any of these.

Although Sunosi has lower abuse potential than traditional amphetamines, it is still a Schedule IV controlled substance and will have to compete with established and low-cost generics like Adderall (Schedule II). Payers will likely require patients to “fail” on multiple cheap generics before paying for a premium branded drug like Sunosi.

BED is crowded. Vyvanse is the standard-of-care here and went generic in 2023. And GLP-1s, like Eli Lilly’s (LLY) Zepbound, are being used off-label for under obesity umbrellas.

Lastly, MDD with EDS (“sleepiness in depression”) is, in my view, a made-up indication that payers could scoff at with cheaper stimulants available.

Other markets noted on the pipeline image are either crowded (i.e., Fibromyalgia and Epilepsy) or undefined (i.e., Shift Work Disorder).

Conclusion

Although I believe that Auvelity in ADA presents Axsome with a meaningful catalyst and a differentiated product, the stock appears “priced to perfection” (maintain “Sell”). Because the stock is priced so optimistically, the potential for a negative outcome (e.g., CRL requesting confirmatory parallel-group data), although low, creates asymmetric downside risk.

Risks to my thesis include FDA approval of Auvelity in ADA and immediate outperformance in ADA market, revenue growth >50% in 2026-2028, pipeline progress that merits premium terminal value assumptions, M&A activity, and partnership opportunities.

data.title: SCHD: Weathering Potentially Higher Inflation Ahead
data.description:
Investment Rating - "Buy"

As follows from the ETF's description, Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD) is an ETF that targets U.S. public equity markets with a focus on dividend-paying mid-cap and large-cap stocks. It's relatively large for its kind (the AUM is over $85 billion), and SCHD is widely discussed on Seeking Alpha, so I decided to take a closer look at it.

What I found is quite interesting, and I didn't see among the latest research articles the angle I found: SCHD is an equity-focused ETF that can protect investors, at least to some extent, from rising inflation expectations. We all see the fast-developing events in the Middle East and the oil price shocks that spill over previous consensus views. All that seems to be forming a new higher-volatility reality for equity investors, where SCHD — partially thanks to its latest recomposition and historical correlation with the broad market — stands to benefit (on a relative basis). So, as a broad-market ETF in its niche, SCHD looks quite interesting at its current price levels, in my opinion.

Higher Odds Of Inflation - Better SCHD Relative Performance

The current situation in the Middle East and the challenges of transit through the Hormuz Strait are strongly affecting the oil market, and the changes there are spilling over into other markets.

Last year in the middle of April, WTI futures were trading at $62-64/barrel, and now it's close to $104. So, the implied YoY change as of today is over 62%. It directly hits the transportation costs for every kind of goods, and we can see from agrocultural commodities that the effect is already hard to ignore. US soubeans futures, for example, trade 12.5% higher year-over-year. Another example is the price spike for gasoline:

However, the rise in CPI is slightly delayed in such cases - usually by a few quarters - because businesses require time to transfer elevated costs to the finished goods they sell. Inventory cycles must reload first as old finished goods with old, lower COGS are sold.

The new macro regime is getting priced in advance, which is why the consensus forward rate cuts from 2025 were replaced by a more cautious view. According to CNBC, officials at their March meeting still expected to lower interest rates this year, but I'm not certain they'll do it as gasoline prices climb so fast.

In the currently changing inflation regime, it is interesting to see that SCHD tends to outperform the S&P 500 index ETF (SPY) throughout the whole period of a rising inflation cycle. The same is true when inflation rates start to fall. We can see this relative dependence when we superimpose the SCHD/SPY pair on the U.S. inflation rate:

It doesn't mean, though, that SCHD is immune to correction during heightened inflation because it's still basically a basket of equities. But those equities tend to have lower valuations and higher dividend yields - they're not usually growing to overstretched price levels when inflation risks subside, but they become relatively more attractive, as history has shown above, when rates are at risk of staying elevated or being hiked. SCHD seems to be a nice place to weather the inflation spike if that's your main assumption for 2H 2026 or 2027.

Latest Changes At SCHD Positions It Well

The historical movements or past correlations are great, but they're not relevant if the ETF's holdings structure is no longer supportive for the main thesis. Looking at the recent changes within SCHD's holdings, I find it great that it has shifted toward stronger cash-flow support, and it has somewhat rotated away from stretched areas.

Instead, we see SCHD rotating toward healthcare, which has been an underperformer for months, but where the dividends have remained quite high. UnitedHealth Group Incorporated (UNH), Abbot Laboratories (ABT), Merck & Co., Inc. (MRK), and Amgen Inc. (AMGN) have become the core positions, leading to almost 19% for the HC sector within the portfolio. This sector is generally viewed as defensive during inflationary periods because people always require medical treatment and pay for drugs even when they are priced higher than usual - it's called price-inelastic demand. And SCHD made healthcare its top priority right at its multi-year bottom relative to SPY:

It might seem that SCHD is overallocated to the energy sector right now, but the ETF's manager actually sold Halliburton Company (HAL) and Valaris Limited (VAL) after their great rallies, so the resulting allocation to the energy book is back below 17% of the entire portfolio. I see Chevron Corporation (CVX) and ConocoPhillips (COP) in the top 3 holdings, but their forward FY2027 dividends yields are 3.94% and 2.87%, respectively, so their dominant roles are fully justified to me.

Overall, I see that no single sector accounts for 20% or more, which is good to see — potential institutional capital outflow from technology could be very harmful, as it's the case for SPY since the beginning of 2026 when SPY's allocation to tech was well above 30%.

For SCHD, it's hard to foresee a large outflow from heathcare, for example, because it's already a well beaten down sector among 11 ones.

Assuming that most tech giants keep investing heavily in AI infrastructure buildout over the next few years, and inflation starts to pick up at some point, the required rate of return will climb and make their ROIC insufficient in the medium term because their CapEx was allocated under the assumption of lower rates. It makes a perfect case for further multiple contraction.

I like a lot of individual tech companies, including mega-caps and hypergrowth once. But I like those companies that will likely show higher ROIC in comparison to their cost of capital, or WACC. I'm afraid a lot of tech names won't be able to do it, so SPY is very likely to keep going through extreme volatility periods. SCHD, on the other hand, looks like a solid alternative as it makes a significant portion of its total returns through dividends, not multiple expansion across the board. As long as earnings in the healthcare, consumer defensive, and energy sectors are resilient — which I think is the most likely scenario as we head toward 2027 — SCHD should do fine, probably better than SPY.

The valuation is on the side of SCHD as well if we compare it to the broader general market. According to Market Chameleon, SCHD is trading at less than 19x earnings, which is 4% below its 3-year average:

SPY trades at 25.5x price to earnings, according to Gurufocus, and it's well above its historical average because of the tech tilt. And even if we narrow down our comparison to dividend-focused ETFs such as the Vanguard Dividend Appreciation Index Fund ETF (VIG) and the iShares Core Dividend Growth ETF (DGRO), we'll see that they're above their 3-year average P/Es by 10% and 9%, respectively. SCHD looks way more attractive in terms of its historical valuation, in my opinion.

Risks To My Views

Even if SCHD used to outperform SPY historically during rising inflation expectations, it might not repeat again in the next quarters. We need to see the CPI driver. If it's energy-led inflation as I'm describing above, then it can initially support SCHD's relative performance, but if it's persistent, equities will have to rerate more broadly (including high-dividend-paying equities). SCHD's portfolio can be hit hard as a result, and the relative cheapness might become even more extended.

One more assumption that I laid out above in my thesis is that the healthcare sector will defend the whole book, as it has already been sold off relative to tech and given the price inelasticity of its demand. But in reality, everyone should keep in mind that company-specific regulatory shocks could translate into portfolio-level volatility. Some of the biggest HC positions might have upcoming loss of exclusivity for their core drugs, hence lower forward-looking earnings visibility.

In addition to all this, if the oil shock doesn't lead to anything serious in terms of CPI influence, my whole thesis might crack — SCHD won't outperform SPY, as the latter will be bullishly repriced on the new assumption of lower interest rates ahead.

Conclusion

It seems to me SCHD's strategic positioning right now is shifting to what we've seen during the most recent inflation shock, when SCHD's portfolio was beating SPY and most other broader market ETFs with a growth-tilt. The same setup is forming now, in my opinion, and SCHD's holdings composition in its latest form should greatly benefit from that. In particular, compared to some of its direct peers, SCHD looks undervalued relative to its historical multiples, which is why I picked it as my equity basket with better inflation protection than most other ETFs out there.

data.title: Kosmos Energy: An O&G Company Still Priced For Disaster After A 180% Rally
data.description:
Introduction

With the US-Iran war, O&G stocks are in the spotlight. Oil, which was left for dead in the beginning of 2026 at $60, has risen 80% and is currently around $100. Even before the war broke out, the Kosmos turnaround was already underway, with production bottlenecks being addressed and significant asset sales. Now, in my opinion, the combined turnaround process and tailwind from oil prices are going to supercharge Kosmos Energy (KOS) stock.

The graph below shows the total return in Kosmos and XOP (the most traded ETF for E&P players) in the past three years. Kosmos has underperformed its peers since 2024, with a notable ~60% drawdown in 2025, as operational failures and falling oil prices sent leverage to 5.6x EBITDA.

In 2026, Kosmos stock started to recover. Year-to-date, KOS is up roughly 180%, and yet it has still only recovered a fraction of what it lost. In the coming quarters, as management shows progress in the turnaround, there is room for Kosmos to significantly outperform the sector.

Before diving into the financial statements, let's start with a brief overview of KOS's assets and operations. As the chart below shows, Kosmos production is anchored in two regions: Ghana, which accounted for 47% of 2025 oil and gas sales, and the US Gulf of America, which contributed 29%. Recently, the Ghana partnership received government approval for license extensions until 2040. In January 2026, Kosmos was awarded two new lease blocks in GoA under the Big Beautiful Gulf Lease Sale 1 ("BBG1").

Most of Kosmos revenue comes from oil sales (~86% of 2025 revenues), but the company also sells LNG and Natural Gas.

I initiate coverage of Kosmos with a Buy recommendation. My target price for the next twelve months is $4.97, an ~84% upside to the current price of $2.70 (upside is derived from a probability-weighted blended target - more details in the Valuation and Conclusion sections).

How did we get here? Why is KOS leverage so high?

2025 was a perfect storm for the company: oil prices fell and assets underproduced. The combined effect was a ~50% decline in adjusted EBITDA, from approximately $945 million in 2024 to $502 million in 2025. The underwhelming operating performance meant that the high debt load of ~$3.0 billion USD was becoming increasingly unsustainable. The graph below shows Kosmos's net debt/EBITDA ratio shooting up to 5.6x in 2025.

Underwhelming production was the main problem in 2025

In Q4 2024, management gave a 2025 guidance of 70,000-80,000 BOE. However, average 2025 production came in at only 61,370 BOE, a 5% YoY decline and roughly 18% below the midpoint of management's own guidance.

The main culprit for the disappointing operational results was Kosmo's largest asset: Jubilee.

Net production fell 30% year-over-year as the field suffered a combination of natural base decline and a series of operational setbacks through the first half of the year, with no new wells to offset either. These included water injection and power generation reliability failures carried over from late 2024, a planned FPSO shutdown in Q1, riser instability following the restart in Q2, and higher-than-expected decline in wells on the eastern side of the field. The silver lining is that Kosmos successfully resumed drilling in the second half of 2025. With J-72 online from mid-2025 and J-74 coming on in January 2026, the Jubilee field is currently producing above 93,100 gross boed (accounting for both oil and gas) - approximately 31,100 boepd net to Kosmos—already well ahead of the 2025 full-year average and a strong foundation for the five-well campaign planned for 2026, which has the goal of keeping the current output stable throughout 2030.

There were also problems in Kosmo's second most important region: the Gulf of America. At the start of 2025, the region had a production run-rate of 18,000 boepd from Kodiak, Odd Job, and Winterfell assets. Management promised investors it would reach a 20,000 boepd run-rate by drilling a new well at Winterfell in Q325. However, this drilling campaign went wrong in quite a dramatic way: severe mechanical failures led the production casing to collapse, rendering it unproducible. The well was abandoned in late Q325, triggering a $51.1 million write-off of the capitalized drilling costs and further impairment charges. However, there is still hope that the Gulf of America can resume growth in 2026. Management is expected to make a FID (Final Investment Decision) in H126 on the nearby Tiberius reserve. The project includes a tieback to the existing Gulf of America infrastructure and has the potential to add a production growth that is not yet reflected in consensus estimates.

What else went wrong in 2025

Production below guidance was not the only problem in 2025.

The Greater Tortue Ahmeyim (GTA) LNG project in Mauritania/Senegal, which achieved first cargo in April 2025, generated a gross loss of $122 million in its first year. This is a structural feature of any large LNG project in its ramp-up phase: the FLNG vessel and operational cost obligations carry substantial fixed costs from day one, while production volumes build gradually toward run-rate capacity.

Oil prices also deteriorated through the year. Brent averaged $69/bbl in 2025, down 14% from $80/bbl in 2024 — the lowest annual average since 2020. With KOS realizing approximately $67/bbl on its oil sales, it meant a $166 million headwind to the top line on its own.

Why do I believe in a turnaround? Recent developments support it.

Since we are dealing with a small-cap commodity producer with high leverage, we can't just buy the stock and forget. It is important to closely monitor turnaround progress in the coming quarters. Recent developments, however, give investors hope that Kosmos is leaving the terrible 2025 behind and moving towards becoming a profitable E&P with a strong balance sheet.

Oil prices—$70 per barrel is enough

The oil price rise in 2026 indicates Kosmos's luck is starting to change. Brent entered 2026 in the low $60s and has since moved sharply higher into the $90-100 range on the back of US-Iran tensions. Brent is the most important benchmark for Kosmos, since it is used to price both oil and gas sales (the GTA contract formula is priced at approximately 9.5% of Brent per MMBtu). My base case assumes a price of $70 going forward—not because I think it’s the most likely outcome today, but because it is the level at which the deleveraging thesis still works.

Jubilee—the main problem in 2025 was solved

As previously mentioned, Jubilee was the main source of operational setbacks in 2025 with infrastructure failures, a planned FPSO shutdown, and an absence of new wells. Each problem has already been addressed by management. With J-72 online from mid-2025 and J-74 added in January 2026, gross field production is already running above 93,100 boepd—approximately 31,100 net to KOS—well above the 2025 full-year average of roughly 20,000 net bbl/d. Therefore, for the turnaround to work, analysts only need to keep an eye to see if Jubilee maintains the current run rate in the coming quarters.

GTA—the biggest earnings swing and the most overlooked

The GTA LNG project generated a $122 million gross loss in 2025—not because the asset is broken, but because that is what ramp-up economics look like for a large-scale LNG project. Fixed costs are immediate; volumes build over time. In 2026, GTA is targeting 32–36 LNG cargos at nameplate capacity, and lifting costs per boe are expected to fall more than 50% year-over-year. The swing from a $122 million gross loss to breakeven-or-better is the single largest earnings driver in my financial model, yet it receives almost no attention in sell-side coverage.

Balance sheet improvements

Kosmos management recently took two important steps to reduce its leverage: the Equatorial Guinea sale and a share offering.

In 2025, Kosmos agreed to sell its 40.375% non-operating working interest in the Ceiba Field and Okume Complex in Equatorial Guinea for $180 million upfront, plus contingent payments of up to $39.5 million. By exiting a non-operated, declining asset, KOS also sheds roughly $100 million in combined capex and G&A obligations over the next two years. The deal is expected to close in Q3 2026 pending CEMAC regulatory approval, with proceeds going directly to debt paydown.

On March 10, Kosmos priced an offering of 97.5 million shares at $1.90, raising $185 million in gross proceeds. The stock fell roughly 16% on the announcement, with investors concerned about a dilution at low share prices. But the market reaction conflates dilution with value destruction. Yes, existing shareholders are now worth a smaller slice of a larger share count. The flip side is that the proceeds directly retire commercial facility debt, removing the near-term refinancing risk that was arguably the single biggest overhang on the stock.

Taking all the recent developments into account, Kosmos will have higher volumes and sale prices in 2026. Together with an additional cash of $365 million from E/G sale and share issuance proceeds, it is highly likely that it will be able to significantly mitigate its high leverage. In the section below, I go into greater detail on cash generation and the deleveraging thesis.

Valuation

The Conservative Base Case

When performing valuation in E&P companies, it is good practice to assume conservative commodity prices. It helps you sleep at night if you know the company can survive even in a low-price environment. In this base case, I assume oil prices fall back to $70 (approximately 35% below the current price for Brent).

In Kosmo's particular case, what matters most is cash flow. The company needs to generate cash at any cost to be able to reduce its debt and take leverage back to healthy levels. Therefore, I made a detailed cash flow forecast for Kosmos in the coming years.

The waterfall chart below demonstrates that at an oil price of $70, CFO rises from $333 million in 2025 to $792 million in 2026. The main takeaway is that the improved CFO is not a result of higher oil prices but a function of the improvements in operational performance detailed above: volume recovery of Jubilee, GTA at full nameplate capacity, and the resulting lowering of cost per barrel. (Note: although KOS reports under US GAAP, I removed the interest expense from the CFO to have a more clear-cut overview of the company's operational performance).

Moving from CFO to the full cash flow picture, the EG sale and share issuance contribute an additional $365 million in balance sheet proceeds. Management guided 2026 capex at $350 million.

Even after absorbing $350 million in capex and $228 million in net interest expense—the two largest cash outflows—Kosmos generates $214 million in net cash in 2026 without issuing new debt. Cash rises from $118 million to $332 Million.

Management pointed out that the cash generated from the E/G sale and share issuance will be used to retire debt. This is important: every dollar of debt retired shrinks that $228 million interest burden. At current rates, paying down $365 million in principal saves roughly $25–30 million annually, compounding the FCF improvement into 2027 and beyond.

With EBITDA recovering to ~$944 million and net debt falling to ~$2.2 billion, leverage drops from 5.6x to 2.4x—moving KOS from distressed territory into a range where E&P peers trade on fundamentals rather than survival odds.

I forecast normalized FCF of ~$425 million from 2027 onward. Note that interest expense reduction is the main lever that takes FCF from ~$400 million in 2026 to the $425 million level.

Against the current market cap of $1,500 million, that implies an FCF yield of ~28%—with oil prices at $70.

The forecasted free cash flow yield is much higher than the overall sector. As of the beginning of April, which accounts for the recent run-up in energy stock prices, the median TTM FCF yield for companies in the XOP ETF is 5.60%. This is a direct comparison to Kosmos FCF in 2027, since in the last twelve months, Brent prices averaged ~$70 USD.

Furthermore, Kosmos is also discounted when compared to its closest peers: Talos and Harbour Energy. In my opinion, Talos and Harbour are the closest peers since both have an E&P portfolio concentrated outside the US. Talos (TALO) has a current FCF yield of 24.4% (TTM FCF of $453.9 million @ $2.7 billion market cap), and Harbour (HBL LN) has a yield of 17.2% (TTM FCF of $1,066 million @ $6.2 billion market cap). Talos trades at a similar discount but enters 2026 with 0.7x leverage and no comparable turnaround story, limiting the re-rating potential relative to Kosmos.

As we know by now, Kosmos trades at a discount because it has earned one: high leverage, recent execution failures, and a distressed equity raise. The re-rating thesis is that this discount compresses as the evidence of a turnaround accumulates.

At $425 million in annual FCF, a 15.7% yield implies a market cap of ~$2.7 billion—roughly 66% upside to the current price of $2.70 (market cap $1.6 billion). I have arrived at the 15.7% yield by calculating the average between the TTM FCF yield of Talos, Harbour, and XOP, assuming Kosmos's leverage normalizes in the next 12 months.

The table below summarizes the full P&L forecast underpinning the thesis.

In summary, if oil prices fall back to $70, the upside limit of Kosmos with conservative assumptions is above 50%, which seems fair for a leveraged E&P operating in frontier markets. Furthermore, the base case represents a floor, not a ceiling. Even if oil falls back to pre-war levels, investors will probably outperform the market by investing in Kosmos, but they also gain significant optionality if commodity prices remain high, which I discuss in greater detail below.

The bull case: what if oil prices don't correct?

My current understanding is that most market participants see the oil rise to over $100 as a result of temporary disruptions from the US-Iran conflict. The consensus is that prices will fall close to pre-war levels after the conflict comes to an end in the near future. Kosmos's current valuation seems to price this scenario, and this is the main reason I choose to use a price of $70 in the base case.

However, it can't be denied that the end of this conflict is highly uncertain, and there might be supply constraint ramifications even if the conflict suddenly ends. Taking this into account, I analyzed 2027 free cash flow sensitivity to higher oil prices. At $100 oil prices, Kosmos can generate $650 million FCF in 2027.

Assuming Brent stays at $100 and considering the peers typically trade at 15.7% free cash flow yield, this means an upside of 150% (considering the stock currently trades at $3.00). In this hypothetical scenario, leverage drops fast, and Kosmos reaches a net-debt/EBITDA ratio close to 0 by the end of 2028.

Given the current uncertain market environment, it is important to keep the bull case in mind.

Catalysts and Risks to Watch

Beyond the Q1 earnings report, which will demonstrate progress in Jubilee and GTA operating performance, two variables are worth tracking independently. First, the EG asset sale close—expected Q3 2026, pending CEMAC regulatory approval—which delivers $180 million in additional debt paydown and removes a key remaining balance sheet uncertainty. Second, the GoA Tiberius FID: if management sanctions the project in H1 2026 as signaled, it adds a production growth leg to the thesis that is entirely absent from current consensus.

Then we have the oil price question: the deleveraging thesis works at $70 Brent, but a sustained move below that level—whether from a Hormuz resolution, demand destruction, or an OPEC supply response—would slow the path to sub-3x leverage and likely retest the stock.

The bear case isn't complicated: another Jubilee mechanical failure, or GTA slipping back below nameplate, would resurrect the execution risk discount the market is currently starting to price out. The stock has earned skepticism. It needs consecutive clean quarters to lose it. A second risk is the balance sheet itself. At 2.4x net debt/EBITDA by end-2026, KOS is recovering but not yet safe. A sustained move in Brent below $65 would slow the deleveraging path materially, and the company has limited flexibility to cut capex further without sacrificing the Jubilee drilling program that underpins the production recovery. Finally, the share offering has not closed the door on further dilution: if operational results disappoint in H1 2026 and oil prices soften simultaneously, management may face pressure to raise equity again—this time from an even weaker negotiating position.

Conclusion

I am long KOS. The position is built on a simple premise: this is not a broken company; it is a temporarily overleveraged one, and the overleveraging had identifiable causes that are now being addressed one by one. The market is still pricing in a level of distress that the 2026 operational data is beginning to contradict. If the recovery holds, the re-rating takes care of itself.

The developments detailed in the turnaround section mean Kosmos has left behind the operational problems in 2025 and is well positioned to deleverage its balance sheet, even if oil prices fall back to pre-war levels.

In the base case, I forecast FCF 2027 at $425 million. At 15.7% FCF yield - in line with the E&P sector- and considering the recent share issuance, which took total shares outstanding to 601 million, I arrive at a target price of $4.50 in the base case.

If oil prices don't fall and remain at $100, FCF rises to $650 million. At a 15.7% FCF yield, with 601 million shares outstanding, I arrive at a target price of $6.88.

Although I understand this is a very difficult call to make, I want to get off the fence and attribute a probability of 20% to oil prices remaining above $100, which reflects the market consensus that the current conflict is temporary. With this probability in mind, the final target price for Kosmos in the next twelve months is $4.97 [(20% × $6.88) + (80% × $4.50)], an 84% upside to the current price of $2.70.
